title: W04 Core Data Structures - Comprehensive Review

questions.question_text: Which principle does the Stack data structure follow?
questions.source_reference: Stack section: 'Follows the LIFO principle: last in, first out'

questions.question_text: Which principle does the Queue data structure follow?
questions.source_reference: Queue section: Queue follows the FIFO principle

questions.question_text: What does the `java.util.Set<E>` interface guarantee about its elements?
options: Elements are stored in sorted order
Elements can be accessed by index
Elements are stored in insertion order
questions.source_reference: java.util.Set<E>: 'unordered collection of objects that contains no duplicate elements'

options: A boolean indicating if the element exists
The element at the specified position
questions.correct_answer: The element at the specified position
questions.source_reference: java.util.List<E>: 'get(index: int): E – Retrieves the element at the specified position in the list'

questions.question_text: Which Stack operation places an element on top of the stack?
questions.source_reference: Stack section: 'void push(Object element) – puts element on top of the stack'

questions.question_text: When implementing a Queue using an array and it becomes full during `enqueue()`, what happens according to the lecture's implementation?
options: A new array with double the size is created
The enqueue operation is silently ignored
questions.correct_answer: A new array with double the size is created
questions.source_reference: Implementation: Queue with an Array – enqueue(): 'we create a new array with double size'

questions.question_text: Which Java class is a concrete implementation of the `List` interface that is based on a dynamic array?
What is the correct term for the concept where a data type only exposes its operations while hiding its internal implementation details?
questions.source_reference: Abstract data types: 'Specify only the operations, Hide details → Information Hiding'

questions.question_text: In the circular array implementation of a Queue, how is the next index calculated to wrap around the array?
questions.source_reference: Implementation: Queue with an Array – enqueue(): 'last = (last + 1) % length'

questions.question_text: What does the `java.util.List` method `remove(element: E)` return?
options: The index where the element was found
true if the element was successfully removed
questions.correct_answer: true if the element was successfully removed
questions.source_reference: Explanations: 'remove(element: E): boolean – Removes the first occurrence of a specific element from the list, returning true if it was removed'

questions.question_text: The `java.util.Set` interface allows elements to be accessed by their index (e.g., `set.get(0)`).
questions.source_reference: java.util.Set<E>: Set does not include a get(index) method in its interface, unlike List

questions.question_text: Both `ArrayList` and `LinkedList` implement the same `List` interface in Java.
questions.source_reference: ArrayList vs LinkedList: 'Both implementations in java.util follow the same interface List'

questions.question_text: A `ConcurrentModificationException` can occur if a collection is modified while being iterated over with a for-each loop.
questions.source_reference: Error handling and common pitfalls: 'Concurrent modification exceptions occur when a collection is modified while iterating over it'

questions.question_text: Which of the following are listed as the most used Java collection types in the lecture? (Select all that apply)
questions.source_reference: Java collection types: 'Most used types: List, Set, Queue, Map'

questions.question_text: Which of the following are stated disadvantages of using boxed Integers in generic collection types (e.g., `List<Integer>`) compared to primitive `int[]` arrays? (Select all that apply)
options: They require more memory space (~twice as much)
They lead to many small objects distributed over memory, causing worse cache behavior
You have to deal with null values
correct_answer: They require more memory space (~twice as much)
They lead to many small objects distributed over memory, causing worse cache behavior
You have to deal with null values
questions.source_reference: int[] section: disadvantages of using boxed types in generic collections

learning_objectives: Implement and use simple abstract data types such as List, Stack and Queue
Explain how stacks and queues work internally
Explain the differences between a List and an Array
Distinguish between List and Array based data types
Use simple built-in collection types such as List and Set
